abstract: This paper quantifies how web scraping distorts measurements of online news consumption in web-tracking research. The authors compare HTML content captured in-situ (via a browser extension during user interaction) to content scraped ex-situ at varying time delays (0, 30, 60, 90 days) for 34,108 news article visits by 412 German participants. They find that the ex-situ collection environment itself introduces ~33.8% content disparity, while temporal delays add only ~6.5 percentage points over 90 days, and that these errors are systematically (non-uniformly) distributed across news categories, biasing downstream analyses.
key_claims: The collection environment (in-situ vs. ex-situ) is the dominant source of discrepancy in scraped news content, not the time delay emphasized in prior work.
Scraping errors are not randomly distributed but systematically biased across news domains, URL-based categories, and content-based (ML) categories, threatening the validity of web-tracking studies.
Most ex-situ measurement error stems from web elements requiring user interaction (paywalls, logins, cookie consents) rather than from textual changes to articles.

Standard bias-mitigation strategies (raising classifier thresholds, dropping problematic domains) fail to correct distortions; only removing the most affected content categories aligns ex-situ with in-situ distributions.
In-situ web-tracking that captures content directly in the user's environment is necessary to obtain accurate measures of online news exposure.
methods: Two-wave web-tracking study (July–December 2023) with 534 German participants recruited via GESIS panel and ALLBUS, using a desktop browser extension (Chrome/Firefox/Edge) to capture URLs, HTML, and timestamps in-situ.
Parallel ex-situ scraping of each visited URL in near real-time using two Python libraries randomly assigned per URL: trafilatura (fixed user agent) and requests (randomized user agents).
Post-hoc re-scraping of last 30 days of wave 2 at 30, 60, and 90-day delays calibrated to each user's original visit timestamp.
Filtering to 34,108 news article visits using URL-pattern heuristics (validated against 1,005 manually annotated URLs; F1 = .937).
Computed normalized Levenshtein distances between in-situ and ex-situ content at three representations: cleaned text (trafilatura), raw text (selectolax), and full HTML.
Three news categorization schemes: domain-based (Stier et al. 2020 typology), URL-keyword-based (16 categories), and content-based ML classifier trained on party press releases (Erfort et al. 2023).
Statistical analysis via linear regression, ANOVA, Tukey HSD pairwise comparisons (emmeans), Pearson correlations, and chi-square tests.
findings: Mean Levenshtein distance between in-situ and near-real-time ex-situ content was 40.1% for cleaned text, 49.9% for raw text, and 71.8% for HTML.
Wave 1 distance (33.8%) was lower than wave 2 (45.4%), suggesting carry-over deterioration from cumulative scraping load.
Post-hoc delay added only 6.5 percentage points over 90 days, with most of the increase (5.5 pp) occurring in the first 30 days and a non-linear, plateauing trajectory.
Trafilatura was blocked by spiegel.de between waves due to its consistent user agent; randomized user agents (requests library) proved more robust.
Discrepancies varied systematically by news type: legacy press and tabloids showed larger gaps than commercial broadcasters and hyperpartisan outlets, consistent with paywall prevalence.

77.5% of in-situ Domestic Commerce pages were misclassified as Non-thematic in ex-situ data; 84% of the top 100 most misclassified articles involved pages requiring user interaction (73% paywalls/logins).
Only excluding Domestic Commerce, Technology, and Non-thematic categories produced statistically equivalent in-situ and ex-situ distributions (χ²(19) = 16.87, p = .599).
contributions: Provides the first direct quantification of the gap between in-situ and ex-situ web-tracking by holding URLs constant and comparing the actual content seen by users to what scrapers retrieve.
Separates and benchmarks two distinct sources of scraping error — the collection environment and temporal delay — showing the environment dominates, contrary to prior emphasis.
Demonstrates that scraping bias is systematic across multiple categorization schemes, with implications for measurement of selective exposure, fragmentation, and media effects.
Evaluates and largely rules out simple bias-mitigation strategies, identifying interaction-required pages (paywalls/logins) as the central problem to solve.

Offers concrete methodological recommendations for ex-situ scraping infrastructure (user-agent rotation, rate spreading, dynamic-content handling) and lays groundwork for error-correction frameworks in web-tracking research.
framing: The paper positions itself in computational communication and media-effects research that has shifted from survey self-reports to web-tracking digital trace data (Scharkow, Stier, Guess, Parry). It extends a methodological critique of post-hoc scraping (Dahlke et al. 2023; Foerderer 2023) by arguing that in-situ content capture is qualitatively different from — and substantially more valid than — ex-situ scraping, and connects measurement error to substantive concerns about media exposure, polarization, and audience fragmentation.
